scenario_summary: NIST CFReDS Hacking Case (Greg Schardt / Mr. Evil). A Windows 98 Dell Latitude CPi forensic image from a 2004 NIST challenge. The suspect installed hacking tools including Ethereal, Anonymizer, and Cain, and intercepted network credentials. Canonical evidence: single reassembled EnCase E01 at images/4Dell Latitude CPi.E01 on the CFReDS archive.
memorization_risk_note: Greg Schardt / Mr. Evil canonical answers (MAC, IP, hostname, email) appear in hundreds of indexed writeups. A passing finding here is not evidence of working forensic capability — it is evidence the model has seen the writeups. The citation + entity gate forces every claim to ground in evidence-present spans rather than regurgitated memory; this dataset demonstrates the gates work, not latent capability.
notes: Canonical evidence is the single reassembled EnCase E01 (not the legacy multi-part DD split). The E01 is the authoritative single-file form published by NIST. Legacy DD MD5 (from context/evaluation §A.3): aee4fcd9301c03b3b054623ca261959a (~6 GB). The E01 has a different MD5 — record both after first fetch. SHA256 = <computed-on-fetch>; update via recompute_manifest.py. Download URL: https://cfreds-archive.nist.gov (see NIST CFReDS archive for current path; URL-encoded space in filename).
